sentences.text: So a group of young girl is approaching the building of the Perkonkrusts.
We are already inside-- as I mentioned-- inside the courtyard.
Jewelry is being confiscated, shouting, yelling.
Beating, of course, beating-- beating all the time.
So one of the people who was particularly cruel, Arnold-- was Arnolds Trucis.
A-R-N-O-L-D-S first name.
Trucis, surname-- T-R-U-C-I-S.
Now, the question is, why do I mention Trucis?
I, I was watching him, thinking of the Latvian w-- word-- not name, but word-- trusis, with an s instead of a c, which meant rabbit.
And I always thought that rabbits are really lovely animals and very friendly towards people.
And this is why I remembered his name, because I, I took this man for a wild beast.
So the girls were driven-- including me, of course-- into the cellar, which was dark.
There was a small lamp overhead.
It was dirty.
And there were signs that people had been-- had spent there the night, because it was rather dirty.
In one corner, there was a so-called toilet.
And we were sitting on the floor.
We were not o-- we were offered neither food nor water, although it was summertime.
A guard was all the time watching us, without saying anything, without asking questions.
And this went on until late afternoon towards evening.
These Perkonkrust people started coming to, to the cellar, one after the other.
And they picked out girls, including me.
Upstairs they were all drunk.
I could tell it easily.
There were-- all had pistols in their hands.
So the so-called officer facing me said, upstairs.
And I did go upstairs because there was no other way out.
It was his-- in his office, he raped me.
He humiliated me.
He tortured me sexually.
And I thought that's the end.
His name was Arajs, Viktors Arajs.
Yes.
V-I-K-T-O-R-S Arajs, A-R-A-J-S.
I wouldn't have known it, but when I was crying and weeping and asking for mercy, he said, you bitch, don't you know who is standing before you?
He said, I am Viktors Arajs, the boss of this place.
And this is how I found out who he was, which actually made no difference to me.
And I was taken downstairs.
We were all sitting.
All the girls were weeping, looked terrible.
Some had torn clothes, dresses.
And suddenly the door opened towards morning.
And the Perkonk-- one-- the Perkonkrust man on guard said, who is here Zelma Shevshelovitz, Zelma?
And I said, it's me.
He said, out.
So I went out and I saw Ervin Henkelmann standing before me.
That is the German officer for whom I was working.
He saved me from the Latvians.
He saved my life.
So my nanny had seen me join a group.
She was following us, which was not far from Stabu [?
Iela, ?] and she saw that we were taken to that headquarters.
So she rushed to Henkelmann, who lived next door, and she told him.
And since he was such a fine, absolutely wonderful person, he tried to save my life.
And he succeeded.
That's the story.
Did you ever see him afterwards?
No.
I never saw him again because I was ill sev-- for several days.
And I never saw any of the girls-- whom I actually knew at least by sight-- never saw them again.
They were all killed-- this I heard from their families.
And later we were taken to work already to another place.
Ervik Henkelmann, I know exactly.
When I was already in hiding, I had such warm feeling of gratitude towards that man.
I, I called his-- by phone, I called his apartment.
I still remembered the number.
And a stranger answered.
I said, could I speak to Mr.
Henkelmann?
He said, no, I am sorry.
He's dead.
He has fallen in battle in North Africa.
So I didn't say anything.
And that was the end of the conversation.
Sorry.
Well, I think my family understood what the situation was like-- in what situ-- in what situation I had been.
But nobody could help anyone in those days.
So again, we were taken to work from that police station, where we gathered in the mornings.
We were taken to work to [NON-ENGLISH] Marienstrasse, which was an SS place.
There, we had to work.
Well that was-- there, we were terribly frightened.
But we were-- we had to work-- we means all Jews and Jewesses-- in the kitchen, supporting the Germans with food.
Their bosses was a nasty old Polish woman, very anti-Semitic.
So she didn't even give us food-- usually, she didn't.
And I, for one, had to peal potatoes from the morning until the evening.
But that was already time when we had moved to the ghetto.
I'll tell you immediately.
Time-wise, it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] October, October because mid-October we were in the ghetto already.
And to that place we were taken by two guards.
A group of people were taken by two guards to that place and back to the ghetto by the same people.
So there we spent long days working hard.
And that's perhaps one occasion which I would like to mention.
There was famine in the ghetto.
The rich Jews li-- had, had lived in Riga, but had, had something.
But my parents had fled from their home, and we were poor anyway.
They were always hungry.
And my little sister and my parents were there.
So I stole, from time to time, an onion or a piece of bread.
I hid it in my-- as people called it-- my lion's mane and in the empty heel of my winter boots, which was forbidden.
You can death sentence for that.
But I risked, because in order to get to-- into the ghetto, we had to pass a so-called control.
And there, only Latvians were there, Latvian policemen.
And they examined you thoroughly.
But they never discovered anything in my case.
So somebody had informed the German-- Scharfuhrer Jacob, the SS man-- that I was taking food to the ghetto, which was true-- if you can call it food, an occasional onion.
And he summoned me to his office upstairs.
So I went.
And I said to my colleagues, who were as miserable as I, well, that's my end.
I was wearing-- it was late summer, so I was wearing, I remember, a nice dress, which was not quite fit for peeling potatoes.
And I was standing near the door.
And he didn't ask me to come in.
I was standing as a poor soul at the door, expecting what he's got to say to me.
And I expected the worst.
And he said to me, we know exactly that you are taking, systematically, food to the ghetto.
I said, which is not true.
And he started yelling at me.
And I said-- it's very hard to believe now.
I don't believe myself when I'm saying this, but this is what happened.
I said-- well in German.
I said-- I'll say it in English.
You can kill-- yes, he said, at 3:00 some of our men is coming to the kitchen to pick you up.
You're going to be shot for that.
And he was yelling.
So I said, you can kill me if you want to.
But don't you dare raise your voice at me.
And that decided, obviously, my fate.
I think he was taken aback by what a little hungry Jewish girl could say to a high ranking German officer.
I think so.
I may be wrong.
And nobody picked me up.
But in the meantime, I had-- I was so prepared for me death, that I took off-- I took off my finger [NON-ENGLISH] ring and gave it to a friend with-- [INAUDIBLE]-- with whom I was working.
So she obviously got killed with the ring, and I survived without the ring.
Oh, in-- from Riga, very many people had [INAUDIBLE] things, which were the dearest most precious souvenir from Palestine.
We used to buy silver, you know, very beautiful.
I still remember the ring.
It could be the, the Polish bosses.
It could be one of the people working, trying to-- trying to do something, to find favors, or what.
I don't know.
And Russian prisoners of war were also working there, and they were extremely hungry.
They were working in the courtyard.
And we saw them through the window.
So I stole German [NON-ENGLISH], which was destined for the German front.
And I took the-- I took the garbage down, the potato peels.
And under the potato peels, I hid the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I showed them to the Russians.
And they understood it immediately.
And then I saw them.
I saw-- I saw them eat through the-- through the window.
And I did it systematically.
I really don't know how they didn't catch me.
I don't know whether I was courageous.
I was so sorry for, for, for those people.
No.
We never talked about death, about being killed.
Tried to be optimistic.
We tried to encourage each other as much as possible.
We said, well, we don't know what's going to happen.
We don't know what the end of war will be.
And hopefully, we will survive.
We have to be strong, and, you know, like that.
German, because we were all German-speaking girls working there.
No.
Older people didn't-- were not taken to work.
My mother was very ill.
And my father was over 50.
They were not taken.
Only younger people or middle-aged people, in the best case, were taken to work.
And my sister, nobody touched my sister.
And then, [SIGHS] one of the most terrible days approached.
So the ghetto was already closed.
We were all gathered at the huge ghetto gate in the mornings.
The gate was opened.
We were sent to work, taken back to work.
This happened every day.
On the 28th of-- if you want me to--
Oh yes, before that.
There were no [NON-ENGLISH] Not as-- that I know of.
Not that I know of.
Yes.
People understand by the world ghetto what they saw in Theresienstadt, perhaps, or somewhere, people sometimes enjoying themselves, dancing, and what.
Perhaps this happened after the great [INAUDIBLE].
But when I was in the ghetto, I have never heard of people dancing or singing, or enjoying themselves.
People were hungry.
Those who were not hungry had lost someone in the family.
There was bereavement almost in every family.
And we didn't know what would happen to us.
So on the--
We were seven people in one room, very crowded.
So my parents, my sister, an aunt with her daughter Shulamit, and her mother-- so seven people, which was not very comfortable.
But there was no other way out.
The relationship was very friendly, very sincere.
My mother made a pudding of potato peels because there was nothing to eat.
We were all hungry.
And then we were already in-- at the end of November, people were in a bad mood because so many people had disappeared that we knew there is nothing good ahead of us.
No.
No.
We knew what's going on at the front, of the Russian-German front-- that the Germans are advancing.
They are taking one country after the other-- about Russia, about approaching Moscow, about Leningrad, et cetera.
That we knew.
Oh, by some people who, who got information by friends, friends of friends.
There was no contact with the non-Jewish world.
There were only a few people who tried to help the Jews in [INAUDIBLE].
I met in the street my colleagues with whom I had studied English at the college.
They turned their head away, and they even didn't say hello to me.
Very hard to tell, because I had been away all the day.
And when I came home, I had only my family.
I never had the opportunity to meet anyone in the evenings, because in the evenings, people didn't go out.
It was dangerous.
So it's only at work when I was in touch with people.
Wonderful.
Outside the family, you mean?
Other neighbors, then, only neighbors.
The relationship was usually very good, very sincere and friendly.
People liked to protect each other, to, to help, et cetera.
She kept coming to the ghetto until the day the ghetto was closed.
And my parents gave her, you know, the rem-- the remaining clothing that was there to take the countryside.
When the war is over, we'll get it back, et cetera.
She, she couldn't come to the ghetto.
The ghetto was closed.
And for coming to the ghetto and smuggling something in, there is a death sentence.
No.
I knew where she was living.
No.
No.
Yes.
Then comes the th-- the 28th of November, the day I escaped from the ghetto.
On the 28th of, of No-- on the-- on the 27th of Septem-- of November, as usually, we returned from work.
It was snowing.
It was very cold.
And it took us-- we were cuing up for the control.
For the-- they would-- usually they checked us on our way home.
We heard people shout, cry.
Outside in the street, we saw people moving their things from one place to another, which was very unusual in a dark, late evening.
I reach my so-called house.
My father was standing on-- in the doorway.
He had already heard that people were being beaten up and tortured.
And he was very worried about me.
I was his favorite daughter.
So when I came in, he asked me how things were in town.
And then a gentleman, who took care of the tenets of the house-- a former very rich jeweler-- came in.
And he said, those people who go to town tomorrow will lose their [GERMAN] It means hunger.
I said, I will lose my [GERMAN], but I am going to town tomorrow.
And to my sister, I said, this is a bad sign.
It means they are gathering the people in order to concentrate them in one place.
And I spoke to her in Hebrew-- a different Hebrew than, than the Hebrew we are speaking now.
And I said, please don't tell our parents.
They shouldn't know anything.
They will not survive if they hear what I'm going to do.
I'm going to commit suicide.
I'm not going to give them a chance to torture me, to rape me again, and to kill me.
I will die the way I find correct.
And my motto was, they shan't get me.
This is what I said to my sister in English.
No reaction.
She, she knew me very well.
She knew if I have said it-- and she was like me.
She was more clever and more intelligent, but we had very similar characters.
I think she, she would have done the same in my place, but she decided to stay on with the parents.
She said, and I'm staying on and sharing the fate of the parents.
So in the morning, I went to the gate where we were gathered every morning.
She accompanied me.
And she knew-- I knew I said goodbye to my parents the last time.
I knew I said goodbye the last time to my sister.
And that was the 28th of November '41?
That was the 28th of November.
Then I came to town.
I was the only woman in the group.
There were no women.
Nobody asked me how come you are here?
Nobody asked me.
It's, again, a miracle.
I reached a place, Albert Street, where I live-- where I worked.
The officer, the German officer for whom we worked was not at home, as usual, because he left early in the morning and came home late in the evening.
Almost never saw him.
It was a four or five room apartment.
No, no.
That was-- That was already away.
That was already away.
This I have-- I have missed that part.
But there's actually nothing I can say, because we were thrown from one place to another.
As a Dienstmadchen.
Only as a Dienstmadchen, yes, we all.
That, I don't know.
I never knew his name.
I understood only one thing-- that he was writing a book, that he was a Baltendeutsche-- a German from, from the Baltic states-- who knew Latvian.
He was writing a book.
And now and again, I, I glanced at the book.
And I saw it is about the war.
And she was a baron, an arist-- an aristocrat.
But he never said-- when he saw me on the few occasions-- he never said good morning.
He never answered.
So it's a very bad sign.
But I did-- never knew his name.
I can't tell you why, because my story makes no sense.
Again, it is intuition, I believe.
I-- one day, it occurred to me, I have to steal the key which was hanging on the wall-- a key of the kitchen door.
The, the apartment had two doors-- a front door and a kitchen door.
The kitchen door was actually a double door.
I don't know how great the distance was between the one door and the other one.
But at least there was room enough for me to stay there.

After having taken the decision to commit suicide, I decided I had to write a, a note to my nanny because I wanted her to know that I was not killed, that I was not dying of rape or what, that I had drowned myself in the Dvina.
A very short note.
And when evening came-- towards evening-- it was getting dark in November.
I took my yellow patches off.
I went to Stabu Street 6, where Vaboulis-- so we have missed a story about him, that he-- we didn't speak about how he came to that place.
Well never mind.
Came to his place, and I was very upset when I rang the doorbell.
He opened the door.
I believe he was a little tipsy.
He had friends there.
There was a party in his house.
And I said, I would like you to deliver this note to my nanny.
And he knew my nanny.
They were acquainted.
And he said, yeah.
Will you wait a moment?
I will come out in a moment.
He went-- I was waiting indoors.
And he said, I don't want my friends to see you.
He read the letter, obviously.
He read the note.
And he said, you are not going anywhere.
These are the exact words.
He said, I'm going to hide you with your nanny.
And I remained there.
Ianis Vaboulis.
Mm-hmm.

I stopped where we-- where I saw him on the platform of the railway station, and we tried to escape him because we didn't-- my sister and I-- because we did-- I didn't think a very prudent idea to start a romance or, or a conversation even with him.
And least of all, I wanted to go to dance with him.
So when the Germans came in, we expected-- every day, we expected the worst things to happen.
When I was working in the old of Riga-- and that was, I believe, the hardest time, physically-- I returned.
And my father said that a tall Latvian had come and was asking for you.
He was wearing a, a portfolio in his hand-- holding a portfolio in his hand, and he asked when you would be back.
And my father told him when I have a dinner break.
And I said to my sister, now I know.
[HEBREW] In Hebrew.
And the man came.
I was very firm then.
I was prepared to die.
There was no other way out.
So when he came, and I op-- I opened the door.
I did it purposely.
I wanted to spare my parents.
And I saw the man with whom I had been dancing at the party.
And he said, I have come in order to-- in order-- I've come in order to ask whether I could help you.
Is there anything you need?
I said, thank you.
I don't need anything.
I'm sorry.
We have to stop here.
The cassette comes to end.
And we'll take over in the next one in a minute.
Good.
